title: Analogue quantum simulation with polylogarithmic interaction strengths by extrapolating within phases of matter
abstract: Simple families of quantum Hamiltonians can simulate general many-body systems at arbitrary precision through the use of perturbative gadgets, however this generally requires interaction strengths spanning many orders of magnitude which scale polynomially in the system size and inverse precision, resulting in physically unrealisable systems. In this work, we show that for non-critical systems these required scalings can be exponentially reduced through classical post-processing, by simulating the model at smaller energy scales and extrapolating observables to the perturbative limit. In particular, we show that both local and extensive properties of thermal states with exponentially decaying correlations and ground states with a sufficiently stable gap can be simulated using gadgets whose interaction strengths scale only polylogarithmically in the inverse precision and the system size. As a key tool, we develop a generalised treatment of the local Schrieffer-Wolff transformation for geometrically quasi-local Hamiltonians over many energy scales, facilitating the analysis of perturbative gadget Hamiltonians without extensive global energy penalities, which may be of independent interest.
summary: The paper shows that the massive interaction strengths typically required for perturbative gadget-based quantum simulation can be bypassed. By simulating at smaller energy scales and using classical Richardson extrapolation, one can achieve high precision with only polylogarithmic interaction strengths, provided the system is not near a phase transition.
main_problem: The physical unfeasibility of using perturbative gadgets for quantum simulation due to the required interaction strengths scaling polynomially with system size and inverse precision.
main_result: Demonstrates that for non-critical systems, the required interaction strengths can be exponentially reduced to polylogarithmic scaling by using classical extrapolation of observables.
method: The authors use Richardson extrapolation with Chebyshev nodes and a generalized local Schrieffer-Wolff transformation to extrapolate properties from a simulator Hamiltonian to the target Hamiltonian limit.
model_or_system: General many-body Hamiltonians (k-local) simulated by simpler families (e.g., 2-local Heisenberg models) using perturbative gadgets with ancillary qubits and energy penalties.
key_observables: Local and extensive properties of thermal (Gibbs) states and ground states, including ground state energy and expectation values of local operators.
important_parameters: Perturbation parameter x (where x -> 0 is the target limit), inverse temperature beta, system size n, precision epsilon, and gadget degree d.
main_assumptions: The system must be non-critical (away from phase transitions) and characterized by exponentially decaying correlations or a stable spectral gap.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the replacement of 3-local Hamiltonians with 2-local gadgets and compares the simulation vs. extrapolation approaches. Figure 2 provides a flowchart of the key results. Figure 5 presents a comparison table of required interaction strengths across different simulation tasks.
paper_structure: The paper first proves extrapolation results for analytic perturbations, then develops a local Schrieffer-Wolff theory for singular gadget Hamiltonians, and finally combines these to prove that gadget properties can be extrapolated to the perfect simulation limit.
why_interesting: This provides a powerful theoretical framework for reducing the hardware requirements of analogue quantum simulators, making the simulation of complex many-body systems more physically achievable.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) A 3-local Hamiltonian Htar = P i hi can be simulated by a 2-local simulator Hamiltonian H′(x) = P i h′ i(x) by replacing each term with a perturbative gadget containing interactions scaling polynomially with x−1 [KKR06, OT05]. (b) Behaviour of a simulated expectation value f(x) := tr[Oρ′(x)] (black curve), for ρ′(x) a ground or Gibbs state of the simulator Hamiltonian H′(x). To ensure |f(x) −f(0)| ≤ϵ, one must take x ≤xsim ∼1/ poly(n, ϵ−1). We show that the same accuracy can be achieved by instead noisily sampling f(xk) at xk ∼xext = 1/ poly log(nϵ−1) (blue nodes) and extrapolating (blue curve) to x = 0.
Figure 2: Structure of the key results of this work, with the main extrapolation results highlighted.

Figure 3: Zeroes (blue) of the complex partition function Z(z) := tr[e−βH(z)], for an analytic family of Hamiltonians z 7→H(z). Zeroes of Z(z) correspond to non-analytic points of the Gibbs state ρβ(z) := e−βH(z)/ tr[e−βH(z)], so that the radius of convergence for a Taylor expansion for ρβ(z) is given by the distance to the nearest zero. In a region where ρβ(z) is analytic (shaded orange), Richardson extrapolation can be performed along the real axis. At phase transitions zcrit ∈R, the complex zeroes of Z(z) “pinch” the real axis in the thermodynamic limit n →∞, preventing extrapolation past these points. A similar problem, where Z is instead viewed as a function of β, is studied in Ref...

Figure 4: Away from criticality, the local expectation value tr[Oρ(x)] (for ρ(x) a ground or Gibbs state) of a geometrically local Hamiltonian H(x) can be approximated by ignoring the variation of the Hamiltonian outside of a region of log ϵ−1 from the support of O (shaded in blue). Thus, for extrapolating properties of the perturbed Hamiltonian, it is sufficient to establish that tr[Oρ(x)] is well-approximated by an analytic function for Hamiltonian perturbations of magnitude ∥Hloc(x)∥∼ poly log ϵ−1.
